title: Reading Comprehension: Function
prerequisites: see how a sentence relates to that point
learning_objectives: Explain WHY the author includes a specific detail (its role), not just what it says.
Common roles: give an example, support a claim, raise an objection, provide contrast.
Reject options that merely restate the detail or misstate its role.

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do -> we do -> you do

opening.builds_on: Seeing how a sentence supports or complicates a main point.

items.prompt: A function question asks what about a detail?
items.answer: Why the author included it - its role in the passage.

items.prompt: Name two common roles a detail can play.
items.answer: Give an example; support (or object to) a claim.

items.prompt: Is restating the detail a correct function answer?
items.answer: No - you must say what job it does.

retrieval_starter.purpose: Recalls that function = the author's reason for including a detail.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: Ask 'what would be lost if this sentence were deleted?' to reveal its role.

questions.prompt: How can you reveal a sentence's role?
questions.answer: Ask what the passage would lose without it.

questions.prompt: Why is 'it repeats the main idea' rarely the role of an example?
questions.answer: An example usually supports or illustrates, not repeats.

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: You can see how a sentence relates to the main point. A function question makes that explicit: name the JOB a detail does - example, support, objection, contrast - rather than repeating what it says.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can state the role a specific detail plays in a passage and match it to the right option.
success_criteria: I can say why the author included a detail, not just what it says.
I can name the role (example, support, objection, contrast).
I can reject options that restate the detail or misname its role.

opening_script.move: Do Now on screen; learners answer from memory, no notes.
Cold-call answers; name the argument's conclusion vs premises.
Bridge to today's question type; state what the stem is really asking.
Move to I do: model the reasoning on one worked item.

i_do.stem:
Passage: Some claim that working from home lowers productivity. Yet at one large firm, output per employee rose after a shift to remote work - evidence that the claim is not universally true.

Question: The author mentions the large firm's rising output primarily in order to

options.A: provide evidence against the claim that remote work lowers productivity
options.B: prove that all firms should adopt remote work
options.C: describe how the firm measures output
options.D: introduce a new claim about employee morale
options.E: explain why some people work from home

i_do.explanation: The firm's rising output is offered right after the claim, as counter-evidence ('evidence that the claim is not universally true'). Its role is to rebut the claim. A names that role.
think_aloud_steps: Main point: the 'remote lowers productivity' claim isn't universally true.
The firm's rising output is the evidence for that point.
So its role is to counter the claim - choice A.
i_do.key_takeaway: Name the JOB a detail does for the argument, not what it literally says.

we_do.stem:
Passage: Vitamin supplements are widely believed to prevent colds. In a large trial, however, people taking the supplement caught colds just as often as those taking a placebo.

Question: The author mentions the trial primarily in order to

options.A: challenge the belief that supplements prevent colds
options.B: recommend a specific brand of supplement
options.D: prove that colds are impossible to prevent
options.E: describe the symptoms of the common cold

we_do.explanation: The trial's result (no difference from placebo) is introduced with 'however' to push back on the belief. Its role is to challenge that belief. A matches.
scaffold_hints: The trial is introduced with 'however' - a contrast signal.
So the trial's job is to counter the belief.

immediate_feedback.if_correct: Right - the trial challenges the belief.
immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: D is too strong; the trial challenges ONE belief, not all prevention.

we_do.stem:
Passage: The new policy has clear benefits. It will, for instance, cut commute times for thousands of workers who currently drive across town each day.

Question: The reference to cutting commute times functions primarily to

options.A: give a specific example of the policy's benefits
options.B: raise an objection to the policy
options.C: prove the policy has no drawbacks
options.E: compare the policy to an older one

we_do.explanation: 'For instance' marks the commute detail as an example illustrating the stated benefits. A names that role; the others misstate it.
scaffold_hints: The signal 'for instance' tells you the role.
That's an example, not an objection.

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: 'For instance' signals an example, not an objection or proof of no drawbacks.

you_do.stem:
Passage: Critics say the museum's new wing clashes with the old building. Supporters counter that the contrast draws visitors in - attendance has risen 30 percent since the wing opened.

Question: The author mentions the 30 percent rise in attendance primarily in order to

options.A: support the supporters' response to the critics
options.B: concede that the critics are correct
options.D: argue that the old building should be demolished
options.E: describe the design of the new wing

you_do.explanation: The attendance rise backs the supporters' counter to the critics. Its role is to support that response. A matches; the others misname it.

you_do.stem:
Passage: The device is marketed as waterproof. In testing, however, it failed after ten minutes in shallow water, casting doubt on that description.

Question: The author refers to the testing result primarily in order to

options.A: cast doubt on the device's waterproof marketing
options.B: explain how the device is manufactured
options.D: prove that no device can be waterproof
options.E: describe the price of the device

you_do.explanation: The failed test is introduced with 'however' to undercut the waterproof claim. Its role is to cast doubt on that marketing. A matches.

you_do.stem:
Passage: Many cities have tried to reduce littering with fines, but enforcement is costly. One city instead added colorful, conveniently placed bins and saw litter drop sharply, suggesting that design can sometimes succeed where penalties struggle.

Question: The author mentions the city that added bins primarily in order to

options.A: offer an example of an alternative to fines that worked
options.B: argue that fines should be increased
options.D: prove that fines never reduce littering
options.E: describe the color of the new bins

you_do.explanation: The bin city illustrates an alternative to fines that succeeded, supporting the point that design can work where penalties struggle. A names that role; the others misstate or overstate.

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.
